candidates.prompt: START II, signed in 1993, is best known for seeking to ban what on intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs)?
candidates.current_correct: Multiple independently targetable reentry vehicles (MIRVs)
distractors: Tactical nuclear artillery shells
Submarine crews trained for second-strike missions
All bomber-based nuclear weapons
distractor_notes: START II focused on strategic delivery systems, not battlefield nuclear artillery.
The treaty regulated warheads and delivery systems, not personnel categories.
START II did not abolish all bomber-delivered nuclear weapons.
candidates.current_explanation: START II was aimed especially at reducing destabilizing MIRVed ICBMs, which could make a first strike more tempting in crisis conditions. Although signed in 1993, the treaty never entered into force and later collapsed in the context of wider disputes, including over the ABM Treaty.

candidates.prompt: Russia's August 1998 financial crisis involved which three interacting events?
candidates.current_correct: A devaluation of the ruble, a default on short-term domestic government debt (GKOs), and a 90-day moratorium on commercial banks' foreign-debt repayments
distractors: Rejection of an IMF loan package, a nationalization of all commercial banks, and the introduction of a gold-backed new ruble
A formal sovereign default on all external debt, withdrawal from the IMF, and a shift to a currency board pegged to the dollar
A state seizure of Yukos assets, a bank run on Sberbank, and a temporary suspension of voucher trading
distractor_notes: The IMF did provide an emergency package in July 1998; commercial banks were not nationalized en masse; no gold-backed new ruble was introduced.

Russia did not formally default on Paris Club / external Soviet-era debt in 1998 and did not adopt a currency board. The default was specifically on short-term domestic GKOs, coupled with devaluation and a banking moratorium.

The Yukos case was 2003 under Putin, not 1998, and voucher trading had long since wound down. Sberbank did face depositor pressure, but the core crisis mechanics were devaluation, GKO default, and the foreign-debt moratorium.
candidates.current_explanation: The August 1998 crisis was Russia's post-reform macro disaster: falling oil prices and the Asian crisis contagion combined with a heavy short-term GKO debt rollover burden. On 17 August the government devalued the ruble, defaulted on GKOs, and declared a 90-day moratorium on Russian banks' external private debt — the 'triple' that wiped out many commercial banks, shattered the oligarchic financial system built since 1995, and opened the way for a post-devaluation export-led recovery that Putin inherited.

candidates.prompt: At the 1986 Reykjavik summit, what issue most prevented Reagan and Gorbachev from concluding a sweeping nuclear disarmament deal?
candidates.current_correct: A dispute over the US Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI)
distractors: A Soviet demand for immediate US withdrawal from West Germany
A US refusal to discuss intermediate-range missiles in Europe
A disagreement about whether East Germany should join NATO
distractor_notes: German troop deployments were not the central issue blocking the deal at Reykjavik.
INF weapons were very much part of the broader arms-control agenda by this point.
German reunification was not yet the issue that dominated summit diplomacy in 1986.
candidates.current_explanation: Reykjavik nearly produced a breakthrough on deep nuclear cuts, but the talks broke down over missile defense, especially SDI. Even so, the summit helped clear the way for the INF Treaty the following year.

candidates.prompt: On 1 December 1991 Ukrainian voters went to the polls in a nationwide referendum. What did they vote on, and what was the result?
candidates.current_correct: Whether to confirm Ukraine's 24 August 1991 declaration of independence — roughly 92 percent voted yes, with majorities in every region including Crimea
distractors: Whether to adopt a new Ukrainian constitution separating powers between a president and the Verkhovna Rada — the draft was rejected, delaying constitutional reform until 1996
Whether Ukraine should enter a renewed Union Treaty with Russia and the other Soviet republics — the proposal was narrowly approved but overtaken by events
Whether to ratify the Belovezha Accords dissolving the USSR — voters approved ratification alongside the election of Leonid Kravchuk as president
distractor_notes: Ukraine's post-Soviet constitution was adopted by parliamentary vote in June 1996, not by referendum in December 1991.
The all-union referendum on preserving the USSR was held in March 1991, and Ukraine's December 1 vote moved in the opposite direction: confirming independence.
The Belovezha Accords were signed on 8 December 1991 and ratified by the Verkhovna Rada, not by the December 1 referendum (which preceded them).
candidates.current_explanation: The 1 December 1991 referendum was decisive for the USSR's dissolution: every Ukrainian region — including Crimea (where about 54 percent voted yes) and Sevastopol — backed independence, and Leonid Kravchuk was elected president the same day. Without Ukraine, Gorbachev's proposed new Union Treaty was unviable, and the Belovezha Accords formalizing the USSR's dissolution followed one week later.

candidates.prompt: Ukraine adopted its post-independence constitution in June 1996, ending a long stalemate between President Kuchma and the Verkhovna Rada. What type of system did it establish?
candidates.current_correct: A semi-presidential system in which both a popularly elected president and a prime minister (responsible to the parliament) share executive authority
distractors: A pure parliamentary system modeled on the Czech Republic, with a ceremonial president elected by the Rada
A pure presidential system on the U.S. model, with the president serving as both head of state and head of government without a separate prime minister
A consociational confederation with rotating ethnic quotas for the presidency
distractor_notes: Ukraine's president has been popularly elected since 1991 and holds substantive executive authority (foreign policy, defense, security nominations). The 1996 constitution did not make the presidency ceremonial.
Ukraine has always had a distinct prime minister since independence; the 1996 constitution kept that dual-executive structure, making the system semi-presidential rather than pure presidential.
Ukraine's 1996 system does not use consociational ethnic power-sharing quotas; that model (Lebanon, Bosnia) was not adopted.
candidates.current_explanation: The 1996 Ukrainian Constitution locked in a semi-presidential design — president and prime minister sharing executive authority — and that design has been a recurring source of institutional conflict ever since. Major constitutional episodes (December 2004 reforms shifting power toward parliament after the Orange Revolution, the 2010 Constitutional Court reversal, the 2014 restoration) all oscillate around whether Ukraine is more presidential or more parliamentary within the semi-presidential frame.
